metadata.summary: Identify threats and opportunities, rate by probability and impact, assign owners with triggers, plan responses, monitor weekly. Every risk needs a named owner and explicit triggers.

content.title: Risk Management
content.summary: Identify threats and opportunities, rate them by probability and impact, assign owners with observable triggers, plan responses, and monitor weekly. The rule: every risk must have a named owner (a person, not "the team") and explicit triggers — observable signals that convert an abstract risk into an actionable event. "Accept" is not a free pass; it requires a contingency budget line equal to the risk's EMV.

why.p: Surprises become crises because risks were identified but not tracked. EMV (Probability × Impact in dollars) provides a relative ranking and seeds contingency reserves — it does not imply precision. A risk that materializes becomes an Issue and leaves the register; double-tracking inflates the register and hides what is actually in flight. Risk reviews must be short (15 min) and frequent (weekly), not 2-hour quarterly exercises.

Probability × Impact scores are subjective; team optimism systematically under-rates probability of own work failing. Black-swan / unknown-unknown risks are by definition not in the register; rely on resilience design, not prediction. "Accept" is the most-abused response: it lets teams skip planning while pretending to manage the risk. Registers go stale within weeks if not actively reviewed; a 6-month-old register is worse than none (false confidence). EMV math implies precision the inputs don't have; use it for relative ranking only.

list.item: Project initiation: build initial risk register before charter sign-off
Stage-gate reviews: refresh probability/impact and trigger statuses
Pre-launch (T-2 weeks): focused launch-risk pass with rollback plans
After incidents: feed lessons back as new risks for similar projects
High-uncertainty domains: new technology, new vendor, regulatory change
Trivial internal task (1 person, under 1 week, no external dependency) — overhead exceeds value
Pure agile teams with strong incremental delivery — short cycles already de-risk; use a "top 5" sticky list
Crisis already in progress — switch to incident response; add lessons to register afterward
Threat — negative risk; probability × impact = potential loss
Opportunity — positive risk; probability × impact = potential gain; use Exploit/Enhance/Share/Accept strategies
Known risk — identified and plannable; goes in the register with a response
Unknown-unknown — cannot predict; rely on resilience design, not prevention

rule.statement: Track opportunities, not just threats; use symmetric response strategies (Exploit/Enhance/Share/Accept).
rule.rationale: Risk registers that list only threats miss half the picture. Opportunities require active responses to capture; an unmanaged opportunity is a threat of lost value.

list.item: Step 1 — Identify: brainstorm from charter + WBS + lessons learned + risk categories (technical, external, organizational, project); each risk needs a quoted source from inputs
Step 2 — Analyze: qualitative P×I matrix for all; quantitative EMV = P_numeric × impact_USD for risks that will feed contingency reserves
Step 3 — Plan Responses: assign a named owner and observable triggers; select response strategy; fund Accept responses with contingency
Step 4 — Monitor: weekly 15-minute review; compare register vs current status reports; convert materialized risks to Issues and remove from register

rule.statement: Every risk must have a named owner (a person, not "the team") and explicit observable triggers.
rule.rationale: Risks without owners are ignored. Triggers convert abstract risks into actionable events — without them, the risk stays abstract until it is already a crisis.

list.item: Avoid — eliminate the risk source (e.g., remove unproven technology from scope)
Transfer — shift to third party via insurance, fixed-price contract, or warranty
Mitigate — reduce probability or impact (e.g., cross-train backup, add automated tests)
Accept — acknowledge and provision contingency equal to EMV; never accept without a budget line
Exploit — ensure the opportunity materializes (e.g., assign dedicated resource)
Enhance — increase probability or impact (e.g., early market seeding)
Share — partner with another party to maximize capture
Accept — take it if it comes without special action

rule.statement: Set contingency reserve = sum(EMV) of all Accepted threats; management reserve sits on top for unknown-unknowns.
rule.rationale: Accepting a risk without provisioning a contingency budget is hoping, not managing. EMV-based reserves give the sponsor a defensible number for the risk budget line.

antipattern.description: Stale risk register never reviewed after creation.
antipattern.reason: A 6-month-old register is worse than none because it creates false confidence. Risks have resolved, new risks have emerged, and the register now misleads rather than informs. Weekly 15-minute reviews are mandatory.

antipattern.description: Using "Accept" response without a contingency budget line.
antipattern.reason: "Accept" is the most-abused response: it lets teams skip planning while pretending to manage the risk. Accepting without provisioning a reserve is hoping. Every Accept must map to a specific contingency amount equal to its EMV.

antipattern.description: Double-tracking materialized risks as both a Risk and an Issue.
antipattern.reason: When a risk materializes, convert it to an Issue and remove it from the register. Keeping it in both places inflates the risk count and confuses status reporting.

antipattern.description: Setting risk reviews as 2-hour quarterly events instead of 15-minute weekly sessions.
antipattern.reason: Quarterly reviews find risks that have already become crises. Weekly 15-minute reviews catch drift while corrective action is still affordable.

rule.statement: Require each agent-identified risk to cite a specific quoted source from inputs; reject generic risks without evidence.
rule.rationale: LLMs generate generic risks ("scope creep", "team turnover") without grounding in project specifics. Requiring a source quote forces actual analysis of the charter, WBS, and vendor list.

list.item: Probability ratings cluster around "Medium" — too safe; require justification per rating ("M because vendor missed last 2 SLAs in Q1")
Numerical EMV implies false precision; always print confidence interval or skip dollar values in agent output if inputs lack dollar-denominated impact estimates
Agent may merge distinct risks into one (loses owner clarity) or over-split one into many (inflates register); constrain via target count (8-15 risks for a 6-month project)
Auto-closing risks based on absence of mention in status reports is wrong — no-mention is not evidence of resolution; require an explicit "closed by [person] on [date]" field
Do not let an agent tag a risk as Accept without a budget/contingency line item — it must propose a specific dollar amount or token-effort equivalent
Pair risk identification with Change Control: any approved change request triggers a risk re-rating pass

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A subagent can scaffold the initial register from charter, scope, WBS, similar past projects' lessons, and a prompt list of risk categories. Use a 2-pass model: first pass enumerates, second pass deduplicates and rates. Keep numerical scoring deterministic via a script — let the LLM produce candidates, the script computes Score and EMV. Human-in-loop required for risk responses (Avoid/Transfer/Mitigate/Accept) because they imply budget and decisions. Schedule weekly automated re-scan of register vs new comms logs to surface drift.

Recommended subagents: faion-pm-agent (drafts register, runs weekly review), faion-business-analyst (derives requirement-driven risks: assumptions made → risks), faion-improver (pulls lessons-learned from done projects and seeds new risks), faion-sdd-executor-agent (links risks to specific SDD tasks via risk_id field).

Prompt pattern: Input charter.md, wbs.md, prior_lessons.md, vendor_list.md; Output risks.yaml (id, title, category{technical|external|org|project}, probability{L,M,H}, impact{L,M,H}, type{threat|opportunity}, trigger, owner_role, response_strategy, response_plan, status). Rules: each risk has a quoted source from input; no invented vendors.
Weekly audit: compare current register against last 7 days of status_reports. Flag: closed risks still listed; new issues mentioned twice but not in register; stale risks (no update in 30 days).

list.item: Track opportunities, not just threats — Exploit/Enhance/Share/Accept symmetric to threat strategies.
Every risk has an Owner who is a person, not a role like "the team".
Define explicit Triggers (observable signals) so risks become actionable, not abstract.
Reserve contingency = sum(EMV) of accepted risks; management reserve sits on top for unknown-unknowns.
Convert a risk that materialises into an Issue and remove it from the register (don't double-track).
Risk reviews are short (15 min) but frequent (weekly) — not 2-hour quarterly drills.
Pair with Change Control: any change request triggers a risk re-rating pass.

section.name: tools-and-services
section.p: CLI tools: yq (filter risks by score, owner); jq (process JSON exports from Jira/Risk modules); python -m statistics / numpy (Monte Carlo for schedule risk (PERT)); pandoc (render register to DOCX for steering committee).

Services and apps: Jira (Risk Register plugin, agent-friendly via REST API — use a Risk issue type with custom P/I fields); Atlassian Confluence (agent-friendly via REST API, common register host); Smartsheet (agent-friendly via REST + automations, triggers when status changes); Riskonnect (agent-friendly API, enterprise risk); ServiceNow GRC (agent-friendly Table API, for regulated industries); LogicManager (agent-friendly API, mid-market GRC); OpenRisk (FAIR, agent-friendly via Python lib pyfair, quantitative risk in dollar terms); Notion / Airtable (agent-friendly via REST API, lightweight registers for solo/small projects).

template.purpose: Risk register table: ID, category, P, I, score, response, owner, trigger, status
Individual risk response plan with prevention steps and fallback actions

script.purpose: Compute Score and EMV from risks.yaml using deterministic P×I matrix
